planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade near permanent water; corn lily is a wetland and moist-meadow plant of mountain streams and high-elevation meadows — ALL PARTS ARE EXTREMELY TOXIC.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; corn lily requires consistently moist to wet conditions and is a plant of high-elevation mountain meadows, stream banks, and seepy slopes in western North America. Veratrum californicum is a dramatic, large native perennial closely related to eastern false hellebore (V. viride) producing equally spectacular, tightly pleated, corn-like leaves up to 12 inches wide on stems reaching 3–6 feet tall in early spring, and dense, white-flowered panicles in mid-summer; it differs from V. viride primarily in its white (not greenish-yellow) flowers and western North American range. ALL PARTS OF THIS PLANT ARE AMONG THE MOST TOXIC OF ANY NATIVE NORTH AMERICAN PLANT — especially the roots, which have caused fatal poisoning in livestock and have dramatic teratogenic effects on developing lambs if consumed by pregnant ewes early in pregnancy. Grow only in areas inaccessible to livestock, children, and pets.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 3–4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial shade at cool higher elevations in the southern Appalachians; Gray's lily is one of the rarest and most beautiful native lilies, limited to a few counties in Virginia, North Carolina, and Tennessee.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall 3–4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5) soil in partial shade; Gray's lily grows naturally in heath balds and moist meadows at 2,500–5,500 feet elevation in the southern Appalachians and prefers cool, moist, acidic conditions. Lilium grayi is one of the rarest and most beautiful native North American lilies, with a restricted natural range limited to a few counties in Virginia, North Carolina, and Tennessee; it produces small (1.5–2 inch), nodding, reddish-orange to scarlet, bell-shaped flowers with spots (less reflexed than the similar Carolina lily) on slender stems in early to mid-summer. Purchase only nursery-propagated bulbs — never wild-dug plants. It requires cool, acidic, moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade and is challenging outside its native climate zone; it is best in cool, humid mountain gardens in the Southeast.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Michigan lily is a superb native Turk's cap-type lily for moist meadows, prairie margins, and stream banks in zones 3–8.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall 4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Michigan lily performs best in consistently moist, fertile soil and is especially beautiful planted at the margins of ponds and along stream banks where consistent moisture is available. Lilium michiganese is a native lily of the upper Midwest and Great Plains tallgrass prairies, moist meadows, and stream margins; it closely resembles the Turk's cap lily (L. superbum) but has smaller, more numerous flowers and a more compact habit. It produces whorled leaves on stout stems reaching 3–6 feet tall and bears 3–10 (occasionally more) nodding, strongly reflexed, orange to orange-red flowers with dark-maroon spots in mid-summer — a spectacular sight when established in large naturalized clumps. Outstanding for moist native plant gardens and prairie restoration plantings.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; false rue anemone is a spring ephemeral that goes dormant by early summer — it is one of the most delicate and beautiful of all native spring woodland wildflowers.

directions: Plant divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; false rue anemone goes dormant by early summer and is a spring ephemeral that should be planted with later-emerging shade plants to cover the gap. Isopyrum biternatum is a delicate, diminutive native spring ephemeral of midwestern and southern floodplain forests, closely resembling rue anemone (Anemonella thalictroides) with its finely divided, ternate, glaucous leaves and white, star-shaped, 5-petaled flowers on hair-fine stems in early spring; unlike rue anemone (eastern), false rue anemone is primarily a midwestern species found in rich bottomland forests from Ontario to Georgia and Kansas. It forms small colonies from fibrous roots in humus-rich, moist soil and is a charming, difficult-to-find native wildflower for collectors of spring woodland plants.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring or plant divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Ozark bluestar tolerates consistently wet soil and is outstanding for moist prairie and streamside plantings in the Midwest.

directions: Sow direct in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Ozark bluestar tolerates consistently wet, poorly drained soils better than the commonly grown Arkansas bluestar (A. hubrichtii) and is the best bluestar for consistently moist to wet sites. Amsonia illustris is a native bluestar of Ozark glades, stream banks, and moist prairies in Missouri, Kansas, Oklahoma, and Arkansas, with broader, more lustrous (illustris = shining) leaves than other amsonias and the same starry, light-blue flowers on upright stems in mid-spring; it has excellent golden-yellow fall color. Like other bluestars, it is long-lived, clump-forming, deer-resistant, drought-tolerant once established, and one of the most carefree of all native perennials; this species' greater tolerance of wet soil makes it an outstanding choice for rain gardens and pond margins.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in well-drained, lean, acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; roundleaf tick trefoil is one of the few native nitrogen-fixing legumes adapted to dry shade conditions.

directions: Sow direct in spring in well-drained, lean, acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; roundleaf tick trefoil is adapted to dry, acidic, shaded conditions and is one of the very few nitrogen-fixing native plants that will grow well in dry, poor, shaded soils. Desmodium rotundifolium is a prostrate, mat-forming native perennial of dry oak and pine woods in eastern North America; unlike most desmodiums which are upright, this species lies flat on the ground with very long, trailing stems bearing rounded, velvety, trifoliate leaves (giving rise to the species name) and producing small, pink-purple, pea-like flowers in mid-summer that are visited by specialist bumblebees. The seed pods cling tenaciously to clothing and animal fur (the "stick-tight" quality that gives tick trefoil its name), aiding dispersal; it is the larval food plant of several skippers and grey hairstreak butterflies. Useful as a shade groundcover in native oak woodland gardens.
